title: Grease on Cotton
stainChemistry: Cooking grease is a thick triglyceride that coats cotton surfaces and resists water — water alone cannot break it down. Fresh grease sits on fiber surfaces; aged grease penetrates and polymerizes into a hard, oxidized film. The oleophilic nature of many fibers (especially polyester) means grease bonds preferentially to synthetic materials.
whyThisWorks: POG (volatile dry solvent) dissolves the grease on contact from cotton. The principle is 'like dissolves like' — a non-polar solvent dissolves non-polar oils. Surfactant then emulsifies remaining micro-droplets so water can rinse them away. Applying solvent BEFORE water is critical — water causes the oil to emulsify and spread deeper.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) directly to the stain. Tamp gently
2. If POG plateaus: apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify remaining oil micro-droplets. Flush with cool water
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for final pH restoration
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + don't panic about size
homeSolutions.instruction: Grease looks worse than it is because oil wicks along fiber faster than water would. The visible spread isn't the full stain — most of it sits in the center. On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn — if the fabric keeps its color, you're safe. Save your energy for the steps that actually matter, which are powder preload and dry Dawn work.

homeSolutions.agent: The oil rule: don't add water first
homeSolutions.instruction: Water and oil don't mix. Rinsing a fresh grease stain first floats the oil around and turns a small mark into a bigger faded one. The order is: powder → Dawn (dry) → wash warm. Also: skip the dryer until the stain is completely gone. Heat polymerizes oil into the cotton fiber — that's what makes old set grease nearly impossible to remove.

If the grease is fresh and still wet on the surface, press a folded white paper towel straight down on it. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Get as much surface grease out of the fiber as you can before the next step. Don't rub — grease spreads sideways under pressure and you'll make the stain 3× bigger.

Generously sprinkle cornstarch, baking soda, or unscented talc over the stain. Let it sit 10–15 minutes minimum; 30 minutes on a heavy stain. The powder wicks oil up out of the fiber before you introduce any chemistry. You'll often see the powder turn translucent or gray where it pulled oil — that's exactly what you want. Brush off with a soft toothbrush or the edge of a credit card, then shake out.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — work it in DRY
homeSolutions.instruction: Put a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the stain — no water yet. Work it into the fiber with a fingertip or a soft toothbrush in gentle circles. Let it sit 10 minutes. Dawn is the single best household surfactant for grease — it's literally what wildlife rescuers use on oil-soaked birds. Dry-work matters: water dilutes Dawn's grease-cutting action right when you want it strongest.

homeSolutions.agent: Warm water rinse + second Dawn pass
homeSolutions.instruction: Now rinse with warm water (not hot) from the back of the fabric. Push the dissolved grease OUT the way it came in, rather than forward through the fiber. If you still see a shadow, apply a second drop of Dawn, work it in dry again for 5 minutes, and rinse. Repeat up to three times — heavy grease almost always needs two passes.

If a shadow persists, pretreat with an enzyme stain remover (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) per the label. Let it dwell 30 minutes. Then launder on warm (cotton's tolerance) with your normal detergent plus a half-cup of white vinegar or an extra scoop of enzyme booster. Warm water dissolves residual oil that cold leaves behind.

Hang or lay flat to air-dry. DO NOT tumble-dry until you've inspected in daylight. Even faint oil shadows will lock in permanently under dryer heat — a common "I thought it was gone" mistake. If you see any shadow, repeat steps 5–7 before drying on heat.

If the grease has been through a hot dryer, is older than 72 hours, or survives two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Grease on cotton, age [fresh/dry-cycled/old], treated at home with cornstarch preload + Dawn dry-work + [enzyme]. No heat applied at home." Pros use POG (paint-oil-grease solvent) and amyl acetate that actually cleave polymerized oil — not available as a household product.

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain to absorb surface oil — wait 10 min
2. Brush off powder with a clean cloth
3. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently into fabric
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat soap application if stain persists
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If grease stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is grease on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
